coredata.dc:title: A sheltering ratio fluorescence composite based on BR-CDs for selectivity detection of GSH
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wan, Yuqi
Li, Shuqi
Wang, Jiahua
Pi, Fuwei
coredata.dc:description:
To distinguish different biothiol species and resolve cysteine interference in fluorescence detection, a fluorescence ratio strategy with shielding effect that specifically responds to GSH without Cys interference was constructed by employing the composite of blue-emitting (B-CDs) and red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs). The constructed BR-CDs composite showed a decrease in red-emission intensity and simultaneous recovery of blue-emission intensity in the presence of GSH, while the ratio of dual-emission fluorescence intensity, FL
440
/FL
620 , showed a superior linear response to GSH in the range of 30–500 μmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.27 μmol/L. This designed strategy exhibited excellent performance in the detection of GSH. The BR-CDs complexes were effective in estimating GSH levels in a variety of food matrices, including tomato, pear and potato, with recoveries% ranging from 94.36 % to 112.02 % and RSD% below 0.41 %, demonstrating the potential of the composites for the selective quantitative detection of GSH.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence detection method
Carbon dots
Sheltering ratio fluorescence
Biothiols
GSH detection

Construction of dual CDs ratio fluorescence composite
Preparation and testing of actual samples
Material and optical properties characterization of R-CDs and B-CDs particles
Performance of BR-CD ratio fluorescence composites and mechanism of GSH detection
Quantitative sensing on trace amount of GSH molecules

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of the weight of p-phenylenediamine on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs (the illustration shows the fluorescence intensity of three R-CDs solutions irradiated by ultraviolet light).

(A) Fluorescence excitation spectrum (red line) and fluorescence emission spectrum (black line) of R-CDs; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (C) Fluorescence excitation spectrum (red line) and fluorescence emission spectrum (black line) of B-CDs; (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) TEM images of R-CDs and (B) B-CDs. Insert: HRTEM images; (C) Particle size distribution diagram of R-CDs; (D) Particle size distribution diagram of B-CDs.

XPS spectra of R-CDs and B-CDs. (A) the XPS spectra of R-CDs. Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of R-CDs and B-CDs for (B, F) C1s, (C, G) O1s and (D, H) N1s. (E) the XPS spectra of B-CDs. (I) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy of B-CDs and R-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs, R-CDs, and BR-CDs. (B) Ultraviolet absorption spectra of B-CDs and R-CDs. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of BR-CDs before and after incorporation of GSH.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of R-CDs at different pH; (B) Fluorescence intensity of B-CDs at different pH; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs at different pH; (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs at different pH.
The specificity of BR-CDs ratio fluorescence composite was investigated. Addition of different amino acids, small molecules and ions.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of GSH and BR-CDs at different concentrations; (B) Linear relationship between GSH concentration and BR-CDs composite ratio (FL
440
/FL
620 ), (C) CIE chromaticity diagram of BR-CDs with different concentrations of GSH.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis, construction and GSH response of ratio fluorescence composite (IFE-based “on-off” sheltering ratio fluorescence specific response to GSH).
Comparison of detection performance of various fluorescence composite for biological mercaptans.

head.ce:title: A sheltering ratio fluorescence composite based on BR-CDs for selectivity detection of GSH

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, School of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
To distinguish different biothiol species and resolve cysteine interference in fluorescence detection, a fluorescence ratio strategy with shielding effect that specifically responds to GSH without Cys interference was constructed by employing the composite of blue-emitting (B-CDs) and red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs). The constructed BR-CDs composite showed a decrease in red-emission intensity and simultaneous recovery of blue-emission intensity in the presence of GSH, while the ratio of dual-emission fluorescence intensity, FL
440
/FL
620 , showed a superior linear response to GSH in the range of 30–500 μmol/L with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.27 μmol/L. This designed strategy exhibited excellent performance in the detection of GSH. The BR-CDs complexes were effective in estimating GSH levels in a variety of food matrices, including tomato, pear and potato, with recoveries% ranging from 94.36 % to 112.02 % and RSD% below 0.41 %, demonstrating the potential of the composites for the selective quantitative detection of GSH.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Biothiols are small thiol molecules, including cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), and glutathione (GSH), that play crucial roles in biological functions. Changes in their serum levels are closely linked to human health (Chen et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2013; Yang, Fan, Sun, Du, & Peng, 2019). GSH, the most abundant biothiol in living cells, is an active tripeptide compound consisting of glutamic acid, cysteine, and glycine in reduced and oxidized forms (Gruhlke & Slusarenko, 2012; Weerapana et al., 2010; W. Zhang et al., 2018). GSH is predominantly a reduced form of glutathione under physiological conditions in the human body, and the sulfhydryl group commonly found in tissue cells and can participate in various redox reactions in the body, such as the tricarboxylic acid cycle and glucose metabolism in the human body, and prevent the destruction of erythrocyte membrane structure by oxidants, thus maintaining the stability of the erythrocyte structure (Bao et al., 2023; Pastore, Federici, Bertini, & Piemonte, 2003). GSH is also a crucial reducing agent in the mitochondria, which can help to scavenge reactive free radicals produced by mitochondria, thus protecting the mitochondria from the effects of oxidative stress. In addition, GSH has promising clinical applications as therapeutic and adjunctive agents in the treatment of a wide range of diseases, including liver and kidney diseases, neonatal diseases, tumors, Parkinson's disease, etc. (Dong et al., 2017; Lu, 2009). It is also widely used in nutritional supplements to boost immunity, combat aging, and prevent tumors.

With the growing demand for GSH in health and nutritional products, advances in its synthesis and purification have highlighted the need for effective quantitative detection methods to ensure quality control in production processes. However, current standardized national methods for biothiol detection are lacking. Traditional quantification techniques, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (S. Guo et al., 2022; Pavlidis, Tsiasioti, & Tzanavaras, 2023), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Ivanov et al., 2022; Tsiasioti & Tzanavaras, 2023), electrochemical detection (Wu et al., 2022), and mass spectrometry (MS) (Guo et al., 2023; Mellinger et al., 2022), provide high accuracy but generally fail to differentiate between biothiols, only measuring total biothiol levels. The complexity of biological sample matrices complicates quantitative thiol analysis in humans and food. Therefore, developing real-time assays that can specifically detect small-molecule biothiols in a cost-effective manner is crucial (Ge et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2017).

Recent advancements in ratio fluorescence composite provide a promising solution to these challenges. These composites enable precise quantitative fluorescence detection while minimizing external interferences such as instrumental errors and environmental variations. (Huang et al., 2022; Liao et al., 2022). Carbon dots (CDs) have gained significant attention for their excellent optical properties, including high quantum yields, chemical stability, tunable excitation-emission wavelengths, and broad absorption spectra (Gan, Xu, & Hao, 2016; Xia et al., 2019). Their biocompatibility and simpler synthesis methods further position CDs as preferred materials for constructing ratio fluorescence sensors (Feng et al., 2022; Li et al., 2018; Mi et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019; Wang, Zhang, Wang, & Dong, 2022). However, despite significant progress, most existing detection methods suffer from cross-reactivity, particularly the interference of Cys in GSH detection, which limits their selectivity and practical applicability.

To address these challenges, this study introduces a novel ratio fluorescence composite (BR-CDs) based on the merging of blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) and red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs). This composite is designed to selectively detect GSH with high specificity, overcoming interference from other biothiols such as Cys. The dual-emission fluorescence system utilizes a shielding effect that enables the composite to distinguish GSH from structurally similar species, providing both high sensitivity and selectivity. As depicted in Scheme 1 , the R-CDs initially quench the fluorescence of B-CDs; however, in the presence of GSH, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs decreases while that of B-CDs increases due to an inner filter effect (IFE). The constructed BR-CDs composite not only achieves a superior linear fluorescence response to GSH but also demonstrates excellent recovery rates in real samples, highlighting its potential for practical applications in food safety, environmental monitoring, and biomedical research. This work establishes a new strategy for developing selective biothiol probes and represents a significant step forward in fluorescence-based GSH detection.

Silica gel (for chromatography, 100–200 mesh) was purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd.; anhydrous sodium citrate (AR grade ≥ 99 %), reduced glutathione (GSH, AR grade) were purchased from Beijing Inokai Technology Co., Ltd.; p-phenylenediamine (p-PD, AR grade), N,N′-dimethylformamide (DMF, CP grade), quartz sand (AR, 20–40 mesh), methanol (AR grade), polyacrylamide (PAAm, AR grade), L-cysteine (Cys, AR grade), homocysteine (Hcy, AR grade), l-lysine (Lys, AR grade), L-valine (Val, AR grade), L-isoleucine (Ile, AR grade), L-leucine (Leu, AR grade), L-phenylalanine (Phe, AR grade), L-threonine (Thr, AR grade), L-tryptophan (Trp, AR grade), L-methionine (Met, AR grade), FeCl3, Zn(NO3)2 MgCl2, CuCl2 and 5,5’-Dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic Acid) (DTNB) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. VB6 and VB12 were procured from ThermoFisher; l-ascorbic acid (L-AA), glucose and fructose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Nitrogen gas (N2, purity ≥99.99 %) was purchased from Wuxi Taihu Gas Company. The ultrapure water used throughout the experiments was obtained from a Millipore water purification system (18.2 MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore).

The R-CDs were synthesized and the precursor quality was optimized according to the existing research methods (Jia et al., 2020). 0.432 g, 0.648 g and 0.864 g of p-PD were fully dissolved in 40 mL of DMF, respectively, and then the solutions were transferred to a 150 mL PTFE-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After the reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature, a dark red suspension was obtained. The crude product was purified by silica gel column chromatography using methanol as eluent to obtain purified R-CDs and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

In short, 1.0 g sodium citrate and 0.52 g PAAM were dissolved in 20 mL of ultra-pure water with intense stirring. The composite was then added to a 150 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and reacted in a 200 °C oven for 3 h. After the reaction kettle was cooled to room temperature naturally, the blue-green solution was obtained, and the purified solution containing B-CDs was collected and stored in refrigerator 4 for subsequent experiments after 3 h of dialysis with ultra-pure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of R-CDs and B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the prepared R-CDs and B-CDs materials were investigated. The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs solution and B-CDs solution was measured by RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the optimal fluorescence emission intensity and the fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths were investigated, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) were used to characterize the morphology and surface groups of the two types of CDs to study the interaction force between R-CDs and B-CDs, the covalent bonding and so on, and 80 randomly selected points in the electron microscopy diagrams of the two types of CDs were used for the estimation of the diameter of the particles, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of dual CDs ratio fluorescence composite
ce:section.ce:para: The dual-emission fluorescence composite was prepared by directly mixing B-CDs solution a with the R-CDs solution. To further understand the effect of mixing R-CDs and B-CDs on the fluorescence of a single CD, i.e., the mechanism of interactions between the dual-CDs, the fluorescence intensities of a single R-CDs solution, a single B-CDs, and a mixture of the dual-CDs solution were measured and compared.

In order to maintain the dual emission intensity with excellent detection performance, the construction method of the fluorescence composite was explored by changing the volume ratio of B-CDs solution mixed with R-CDs solution. That is, a 100 μL of dialysis-purified B-CDs solution (69.40 mg/mL) was diluted with 9.90 mL of ultrapure water. Set this solution aside. Preparing a mixed solution of R-CDs and B-CDs with a fixed total volume of 450 μL following the ratios between B-CDs (50, 100, 150, 250 and 300 μL) and R-CDs accordingly (400, 350, 300, 200 and 150 μL). The fluorescence intensity ratio of B-CDs to R-CDs (FL
440
/FL
620 ) was optimized, and a ratio of approximately 3:5 was selected for constructing the fluorescence composite.

Aqueous solutions were mixed with 100 μL of B-CDs solution to measure the change in fluorescence emission intensity at different pH values (3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, and 12). Phosphate buffer solutions were used to adjust the pH values. 200 μL of R-CDs solution was mixed with 1 mL of water at different pHs in the same way to measure the change in fluorescence intensity value. The experiment was repeated three times for each group. In order to determine the time when the reaction between the mixed composite and GSH reached equilibrium and improve the sensitivity and accuracy of the fluorescence composite with double a CDs ratio, the incubation time of the detected substance was optimized by a single factor by adding 1.0 mL of GSH solution, and the ratio of fluorescence intensity of the double-emission composite after adding GSH reaction for a period of time was recorded. The reaction detection time was set to 0, 1, 3, 5, 10, 13, 15, and 20 min. All experiments were repeated three times.

A 1 mL of GSH solution with different concentrations (30, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 350, and 500 μmol/L) were added into the double CDs ratio fluorescence composite system and fully oscillated for 5 min until the reaction was completed. The fluorescence intensity of the composite system was measured by RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The instrument parameters were set as: grating size 10 × 10 nm, sensitivity low. The standard curve between fluorescence intensity ratio FL
440
/FL
620 and GSH concentration was established, and the relationship range and coefficient of determination (R
2 ) were determined. The experiments above limit of detection (LOD) were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and testing of actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh, ripe tomatoes, pears, and potatoes were selected and rinsed with water to remove surface dust. The surface was then dried with filter paper, and a specified amount of each sample (e.g., 10 g) was weighed. Set the samples into a homogenizer and adding an appropriate amount of cooled phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.10 M, pH 7.40) for making homogeneous slurry. The prepared slurry was transferred to a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10–15 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was collected as a test sample for measurements.

Spiking recovery experiments of actual samples were performed by adding different concentrations of GSH to the samples. The composite and the GSH aqueous solution were mixed according to the optimized experimental parameters, and the fluorescence signals of FL
440
/FL
620 were recorded. The concentration of GSH in the spiked samples was calculated using established standard curve and fluorescence signals of FL
440
/FL
620 , meanwhile, the spiked recovery (Re) was calculated.

In this study, the R-CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal process under 200 °C. Considering significant effects of the types and amounts of precursor on the photoluminescence of CDs, it was very important to explore the amounts of two precursors for the optical properties of synthesized R-CDs. From Fig. 1 , it can be observed that with a fixed volume of 40 mL of DMF, the amount of p-PD used affects the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized R-CDs. The R-CDs synthesized with 0.648 g of p-PD exhibit the highest fluorescence intensity. The inset in the upper right corner shows photographs of R-CDs synthesized with three different masses of p-PD under UV irradiation. It can be seen that the unpurified R-CDs emit a dark red fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp, with the strongest fluorescence observed for the sample synthesized with 0.648 g of p-PD. The effect of ligand dosage on fluorescence intensity is evident. Therefore, 0.648 g of p-PD is chosen as the optimal synthesis mass for R-CDs using aniline as the ligand.

Next, the effects of reaction temperature and reaction time were examined. As shown in Figs. S1A and 1B, the optimal emission positions of R-CDs synthesized at different temperatures were slightly shifted, but the fluorescence intensity was the strongest at 200 °C. Considering the laboratory safety, we chose 200 °C as the optimal reaction temperature. For the examination of the reaction time (Figs. S1C and D), 5 h was chosen as the optimal time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material and optical properties characterization of R-CDs and B-CDs particles
ce:section.ce:para: The optical property is one of the most important characteristics of carbon quantum dots materials. As shown in Fig. 2A and B, the purified R-CDs has the best excitation wavelength at 480 nm and a single fluorescence emission peak at 620 nm. With the change of excitation wavelength from 400 nm to 560 nm, the fluorescence emission of R-CDs peaked at 620 nm which is unchangeable, indicating an excitation wavelength independence. However, as shown in Fig. 2C and D, the B-CDs display an independent excitation wavelength at 440 nm and indicates the best emission intensity at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Thus, although the R-CDs display a dependent excitation shift, changing the excitation wavelength has no effect on the emission site after mixing two kinds of probes to construct ratio fluorescence under suitable design.

The morphology of the prepared reference R-CDs and B-CDs was observed with TEM technique. As shown in Fig. 3 , the R-CDs displayed uniform particles with a diameter of 1.75 ± 0.05 nm, while the B-CDs had an average diameter of 3.75 ± 0.05 nm. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images revealed that the lattice spacing of these two types of carbon dots was slightly different, with the lattice spacing for the R-CDs being 0.28 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of the graphitic carbon (D. Li et al., 2021), while that of the B- CDs have an insignificant lattice spacing and may be disordered or short-range ordered.

To understand the optical properties of B-CDs and R-CDs particles, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the two CDs were recorded. As plotted in Fig. 4A, the B-CDs display an obvious UV absorption peak at 340 nm, which is related to the formation of the surface state of excited defects. R-CDs has an obvious absorption peak at 420 nm, which results from the n-π * transition of CO bond (Liang, Han, Liu, Ju, Li, & Luo, 2019; Zhao, Song, Huang, Yang, Wang, & Zhao, 2021). Evidently, the absorption spectrum of R-CDs overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of B-CDs (Fig. 2), so it is speculated that there may be FRET or IFE between B-CDs and R-CDs.

XPS analysis was conducted on the CDs, revealing that they all contained the same elements: i.e., C, N, and O. However, the relative concentrations of these elements varied between B-CDs and R-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 4) showed that both CDs contained C and O, with the most notable difference being in nitrogen content. B-CDs exhibited a minimal amount of N, with a single peak at 398.8 eV (Fig. 4D). In contrast, R-CDs contained pyridine N, which plays a key role in specifically recognizing GSH. After freeze-drying, R-CDs and B-CDs were ground and characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As can be seen in Fig. 4B, the B-CDs has a wide characteristic absorption peak at about 3439 cm−1, indicating that there are-OH and-NH2 groups on its surface. The characteristic absorption peak at 1587 cm−1 is related to the asymmetric stretching vibration of COO- or the bending vibration of NH, and the other two spiking peaks at about 1399 cm−1 and 1076 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of CN and the bending vibration of OH, respectively. Therefore, it is speculated that there are-NH2, -OH and -COOH groups on the surface of B-CDs, which make B-CDs have good water solubility. In the infrared spectrum of R-CDs, the characteristic absorption peak at 3000–3600 cm−1 is related to the tensile vibration of N-H/O-H on its surface, and the spikes of about 1642 cm−1, 1391 cm−1 and 1148 cm−1 can be attributed to CO, CN and CO groups, respectively. The FT-IR and XPS results indicate that the presence of functional hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs greatly improves the water solubility and stability of the obtained CDs, as well as the specific recognition of GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of BR-CD ratio fluorescence composites and mechanism of GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the interaction between fluorescence composites (BR-CDs), their optical properties were evaluated. The absolute quantum yield of BR-CDs was first measured with integrating sphere method, yielding a value of 18.55 %, which is significantly comparable to previously reported CDs. Next, the fluorescence intensities of the BR-CDs were measured as shown in Fig. 5A. Under constant concentration, the fluorescence emission intensity of the mixed B-CDs and R-CDs was significantly reduced but not completely quenched, while the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs remained unchanged. In the case of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between two types of carbon dots, the donor's fluorescence intensity would decrease as energy is transferred, and the fluorescence intensity of the acceptor would increase. Based on such principles, a FRET process may occur between the two types of CDs. Moreover, since the emission spectrum of B-CDs (Fig. 2C) overlaps with the UV absorption spectrum of R-CDs (Fig. 5B), it is hypothesized that the weakening of B-CDs fluorescence is due to the inner filter effect (IFE). (Ravikumar, Panneerselvam, & Radhakrishnan, 2017; Sheng et al., 2020; Zhang, Zhou, Tang, Hou, & Wu, 2019).

The response of B-CDs to GSH was also tested for more information about the energy transfer (Fig. S2). The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs remained unchanged upon adding different concentrations of GSH. When the analyte GSH is present in the system (Fig. 5C), the high content of pyridine N on the surface of R-CDs interacts strongly with GSH, which results in a significant red-shift in the UV absorption and quenching of the fluorescence emission of R-CDs. The photoluminescence (PL) lifetimes of BR-CDs before and after the addition of GSH (Fig. S3, Table S1) indicate that the lifetimes of BR-CDs before and after the addition of GSH were 9.93 ns and 9.85 ns, respectively, with no significant change observed. This observation further supports the occurrence of an IFE effect in our BR-CDs composites.

It is well known that the pH value of the system is an important factor affecting the fluorescence intensity of CDs, which further affects the response performance of CDs. The fluorescence intensities of the two CDs under different pH are shown in Fig. 6 . R-CDs has good pH stability in the range of 3–12, can maintain excellent fluorescence properties in strong acid or strong alkali atmosphere, and has little difference and fluctuation of fluorescence intensity in different pH environments, so it can be used for the detection of various systems with different acidity and alkalinity. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs remains stable within the pH range of 4–12, with little effect from strong alkalinity. However, when the pH drops below 4, the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly. Therefore, for accurate sample detection, the pH of the sample solution should be maintained between 4 and 12 to ensure stable fluorescence intensity of R-CDs. Hence, pH = 7 was selected as the detected pH in the follow-up experimental study.

The incubation time and the target may affect the fluorescence intensities of the fluorescence BR-CDs composites. To ensure the uniformity and the sufficient reaction between targets and BR-CDs composites, fluorescence intensities of the ratio fluorescence composite within 20 min after the addition of targets were measured. As shown in Fig. S4, the dual fluorescence peak ratios of FL
440
/FL
620 changed significantly within 1 min after the addition of 500 μL of GSH, and then the ratio equilibrated. Such results indicate that BR-CDs composites can complete the reaction with GSH within 1 min, holding a superior rapid response. To ensure the sufficient reaction, 3 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time for the system. In addition, no precipitated or floating particles were observed even after three months of placement, and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs showed negligible changes, suggesting that the prepared BR-CDs composites exhibited good fluorescence stability (Fig. S5).

To evaluate the selectivity of BR-CDs ratio fluorescence composites for biothiols and their abilities to distinguish between Cys and GSH, we conducted selectivity studies using eight essential amino acids, common ions, small molecules, and biothiols (Cys and Hcy) commonly found in food supplements. As shown in Fig. 7 , the FL
440
/FL
620 ratios of the BR-CDs composite increased significantly in the presence of GSH compared to the blank control. However, the addition of other amino acids caused no significant change, demonstrating the composite's excellent selectivity for GSH. Notwithstanding the Cys also caused a slight increase in the FL
440
/FL
620 ratio, it is a non-essential amino acid and is rarely used as an ingredient in health supplements. The strong interaction between the high pyridine nitrogen on the surface of R-CDs and GSH leads to a significant redshift in UV absorption quenching the fluorescence emission. The UV absorption peaks of R-CDs overlap with those of B-CDs, but when GSH is added, the fluorescence emission of B-CDs, which was previously quenched by R-CDs through IFE effect, is restored. This restoration occurs because GSH disrupts the overlap, weakening the IFE and enabling the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs to recover. In contrast, other biothiols, such as Cys and Hcy, exhibit weak interactions with pyridine nitrogen and do not significantly influence the fluorescence response. As a result, our ratio fluorescence composite effectively distinguishes GSH from the biothiols, which provides a promising tool for GSH-responsive fluorescence sensing and selective detection in complex systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative sensing on trace amount of GSH molecules
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 8 under the optimal detection parameters, the fluorescence intensity of the BR-CDs composite at 620 nm decreased continuously, and the fluorescence peak at 440 nm rebounded continuously with the increase in target concentration. The BR-CDs composite showed excellent response performance to GSH. The linear fitting of the FL
440
/FL
620 values to the GSH concentration yielded a superior linear relationship between the FL
440
/FL
620 values and the GSH concentration, with a linear range of 30–500 μmol/L with R
2 = 0.99. To determine the LOD of our BR-CDs ratio fluorescence composite, the blank signal of the composite was measured 15 times, and the LOD was 5.27 μmol/L, which was lower than the limit of quantification (LOQ) of the composite, and thus the constructed standard curve had the reliability of detection. Thereafter, we examined the response of R-CDs single emission probes to detect different concentrations of GSH. Compared to the dual-emission composite, single emission performed poorly at low concentration responses (Fig. S6). Compared with previously reported fluorescence detection techniques regarding the detection of small molecules in biothiols, the BR-CDs ratio fluorescence composite in this study showed a wider detection range and the ability to specifically differentiate between GSHs in a variety of biothiols, which is promising for application. Table 1 compares the detection range and detection specificity of the previously reported fluorescence composite and the fluorescence composite in this experiment, which shows the excellent recognition and detection performance of the ratio fluorescence composite. The CIE chromatogram in Fig. 8C shows the continuous color change under UV light. Where the coordinates move from orange to violet, proving that the naked eye can capture the hue change, showing great potential for fluorescence visualization detection.

The BR-CDs ratio fluorescence composite system was employed to detect GSH in various real samples, such as tomatoes, pears and potatoes samples (T able S2). The observed recoveries % fell within the range of 94.38 % to 112.01 %, with relative standard deviation (RSD) % not exceeding 5.05 %. Moreover, the fluorometric method was compared with the DNTB colorimetric method (Özyürek et al., 2012) for the determination of GSH. The analysis concluded that there was no significant difference between the two methods. These findings underscore the reliability of the fluorescence composite for accurately detecting GSH in real-world samples.

In this study, a sheltering ratio fluorescence sensor based on the fluorescence “on-off” strategy was constructed. This sensor enables quantitative detection by monitoring the decrease in red emission and recovery of blue emission intensity in the presence of GSH, achieved through a simple physical mixture and potential IFE effects between B-CDs and R-CDs. Under optimal conditions (pH 7, 5 min reaction time), the fluorescence intensity ratio (FL
440
/FL
620 ) of the BR-CDs composite exhibited excellent linearity (R
2 = 0.99) for GSH detection in the range of 30–500 μmol/L, with an LOD of 5.27 μmol/L. The selectivity experiments showed that the BR-CDs ratio fluorescence composite could distinguish GSH from other small biothiol molecules well, and did not produce signal response to other amino acids, with good specificity, so it has better practical application value than other fluorescence probes for quantifying the total amount of biothiols. In conclusion, a sheltering ratio fluorescence assay with rapid response time, simple operation and low detection cost was established, which can be used for the quantification of active ingredients in food supplements and the quantitative monitoring of GSH content in the production process.

Yuqi Wan: Writing – original draft, Validation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shuqi Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Data curation. Jiahua Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Fuwei Pi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Joint Funds of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U24A20473).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fig. S1 Effect of reaction (A, B) temperature and (C, D) time on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs
Fig. S2. Fluorescence intensity of B-CDs after addition of different concentrations of GSH
Fig. S3. PL decay spectra and and fitting curves of of BR-CDs before (A) and after (B) the addition of GSH
Table S1. Photoluminescent lifetimes of BR-CDs before and after the addition of GSH
Fig. S4 FL440/FL620 values at different times after addition of GSH to BR-CDs
Fig. S5 Temporal stability of BR-CDs
Fig. S6. (A) Fluorescence spectra of GSH and R-CDs at different concentrations; (B) Linear relationship between GSH concentration and R-CDs probe (FL620 )
Table S2. Standard addition test for actual samples

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.143337.
